title: Common Mistakes and Final Recap
description: Learn common process violations and the golden rules that ensure successful Salesforce execution.

sections.title: Common Mistakes and Final Recap
sections.content:
You know the process. You've seen it in action. Now let's look at what breaks it—and why.

The pressure is real. Deal urgency. Year-end targets. Competing priorities. In those moments, it's tempting to cut corners. Understanding the consequences of each shortcut will help you stay disciplined.

Mistake 1: Qualify Before Verification

The shortcut: Skip verification. Go straight from lead creation to qualification.

Why it fails:
- Unverified leads are often dead: contact left the company, company doesn't exist, email is fake, no decision authority.
- You waste time chasing ghosts.
- Your pipeline data becomes polluted.
- Verification takes 15 minutes. Chasing bad leads takes weeks.

What to do: Always verify first. One phone call. Confirm company is real, contact is real, need is real. Then qualify.

Mistake 2: Skip Leads, Create Opportunities Directly

The shortcut: "I know this company already. I'll just create an opportunity directly on the account."

Why it fails:
- You lose visibility into new prospect sources.
- You can't measure lead-to-opportunity conversion.
- Duplicate detection gets bypassed. You might create a second record for an existing contact.
- The structured qualification process gets skipped, so bad opportunities flow into the pipeline.
- You can't report on lead quality or source effectiveness.

What to do: Lead discipline is non-negotiable. Create a lead for every new prospect. It's the foundation.

Mistake 3: Wrong Account Hierarchy During Conversion

The shortcut: Convert the lead to the Parent Account instead of creating an Entity Child Account.

Why it fails:
- Revenue gets attributed to the parent company, not the division that's actually using the product.
- Multiple divisions become indistinguishable in the system.
- You can't see which business unit is driving value.
- Accounting and reporting break down.

What to do: Take time during conversion. Determine the right Parent Account and Entity Child Account. Ask for help from the CRM team if you're unsure. Hierarchy matters.

Mistake 4: Duplicate Accounts, Contacts, or Opportunities

The shortcut: Create a new account without checking if it already exists.

Why it fails:
- Your team pursues the same deal twice instead of focusing on one opportunity.
- The customer gets contacted twice about the same issue.
- Revenue gets double-counted (two opportunities for one deal = inflated forecast).
- The database becomes bloated and confusing.
- Account managers lose visibility into the full relationship.

What to do: Always check for duplicates before creating records. Review the system's duplicate matching results carefully. If you're unsure, ask the CRM team to investigate first.

Mistake 5: Advance Opportunity Without Meeting Stage Gates

The shortcut: Move an opportunity to Pricing & Quotation without confirming scope or primary contact. "Show progress."

Why it fails:
- Stage gates protect against missing critical information.
- Quotes sent before scope is locked create complex negotiations and revisions.
- Forecasts become unreliable because stages don't reflect deal maturity.
- Leadership makes bad decisions based on false signals ("deal almost done" when it needs 2 more months).

What to do: Respect the gates. Don't advance until you've met the criteria. If a gate seems wrong, discuss it with your manager and document the exception.

Mistake 6: Send Quote Without Approval

The shortcut: Prepare a quote and send it directly to the customer.

Why it fails:
- Pricing errors slip through (user priced at $5K when it should be $8K).
- Unauthorized discounts get promised without company approval.
- Special terms (liability, data rights) skip legal review.
- If the customer questions pricing later, there's no approval trail.

What to do: Always route quotes through the approval process. Wait 1-2 days for sign-off. This prevents expensive mistakes.

Mistake 7: Quote and Contract Are Not the Same

The shortcut: Send a quote with a signature block, expecting it to serve as a contract.

Why it fails:
- Quotes are proposals. They're not legally binding.
- Signing a quote doesn't create a binding contract. Ambiguity emerges later.
- When a real contract comes, it may conflict with the quote, creating legal confusion.
- Revenue recognition becomes uncertain (can you recognize revenue on a signed quote? Or must you have a formal contract?).
- In disputes, the enforceability of quoted terms gets challenged.

What to do: Use quotes for proposals and negotiations. Once the customer accepts the quote, prepare a formal contract with full legal language. Link them together in Salesforce for traceability.

Mistake 8: Close Won Without Signed Acceptance

The shortcut: Close the opportunity as Won the moment the customer verbally agrees or sends an email saying "let's move forward."

Why it fails:
- Verbal agreements aren't binding. Customers change their minds (budget gets cut, they pick a competitor, priorities shift).
- Revenue can't be recognized without a signed contract or written acceptance.
- If the customer disputes later, you have no proof they agreed.
- Your forecast becomes fiction (deals marked Won that never actually close).

What to do: Close as Won only when:
- A contract is signed by authorized parties on both sides, OR
- Written acceptance exists (signed PO, signed order confirmation, or explicit email acceptance)

Always get it in writing.

Mistake 9: Leave Reason Fields Blank

The shortcut: Close the opportunity as Won or Lost without filling in the Reason field.

Why it fails:
- Win/loss analysis becomes impossible. You can't identify patterns.
- You can't distinguish competitive loss (product problem) from budget loss (market issue) from churn (customer success problem).
- Coaching is guesswork. You can't teach reps how to win if you don't know why they're losing.
- Continuous improvement stops.

What to do: Always fill in Reason fields. Be specific: "Competitor won on price," "Budget didn't get board approval," "Customer merged with a competitor." This data is gold.

The Seven Golden Rules

Follow these and you protect the process, your team, and the company.

#Golden Rule 1: Start with Lead
Every prospect starts as a lead. No exceptions. Create it, verify it, qualify it, convert it. Lead discipline is the foundation.

#Golden Rule 2: Qualify with Evidence
Don't mark a lead qualified because it feels right. Verify all five criteria: valid company, valid contact, defined need, defined budget, defined timeline. Document the evidence.

#Golden Rule 3: Convert Correctly
Determine the right Parent Account and Entity Child Account during conversion. Ask for help if you're unsure. Correct hierarchy is non-negotiable.

#Golden Rule 4: Sell on Entity Child
All opportunities go on the Entity Child Account, never the Parent. This ensures accurate revenue attribution and clean reporting.

#Golden Rule 5: One Opportunity per Deal
Create one opportunity per distinct commercial engagement. Don't combine multiple deals. Don't duplicate the same deal.

#Golden Rule 6: Quote ≠ Contract
Quotes are proposals. Contracts are binding agreements. Use each for its purpose. Link them for traceability.

#Golden Rule 7: Keep Data Clean
Always check for duplicates. Always complete required fields. Always document reason fields. Always link related records. Data quality is everyone's job.

You're Ready

You've completed the NxN Salesforce Commercial Process Training. You understand:
- The journey from lead to active customer
- Who owns what at each stage
- The gates and disciplines that protect the process
- The systems that support it
- The mistakes that break it

The process evolves as NxN grows, but when executed consistently, it delivers three outcomes:

1. Consistency: Everyone follows the same process, uses the same system, speaks the same language.
2. Control: Governance checkpoints and approvals prevent errors and unauthorized commitments.
3. Visibility: Clean data enables accurate reporting, forecasting, and smart decisions.

Your role—whether sales rep, account manager, approver, or operations—is to follow this process with discipline, do excellent work, and maintain system integrity.

When you do that, you make the whole organization stronger.

Let's build great customer relationships and grow this business. Welcome to the NxN commercial team.

keyTakeaways: Golden Rules: Start with Lead, Qualify with Evidence, Convert Correctly, Sell on Entity Child, Keep Data Clean
Never skip verification, create opportunities directly on accounts, bypass stage gates, or send quotes without approval
Always fill in reason fields (win/loss, churn) to enable win/loss analysis and continuous improvement
